text.title: decimal-as-string-pattern Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for declaring exact-form fields as pattern-constrained strings.

rule.statement: For currency, exact decimals, big integers (> 2^53), structured identifiers, and strict timestamps, declare the schema field as a string with a regex `pattern` that pins the exact surface form, then parse to Decimal/int/datetime on the receiving side.
rule.rationale: JSON number types do not pin precision; float deserialization can introduce binary-rounding drift. Regex-constrained strings are masked at decode time on strict-mode and grammar backends, so the model literally cannot emit "19.989999" when the pattern demands two decimal places.

rule.statement: Pick the strictest pattern that the legitimate input distribution can satisfy; pair it with a Field `description` that names the format and gives one canonical example.
rule.rationale: Patterns enforce at decode time; the description anchors the model in JSON-mode-only fallbacks. The pattern catches drift; the description teaches the format on the first try.

rule.statement: Consumer code MUST parse the validated string to the exact native type (Decimal, int, datetime) immediately after Pydantic validation; arithmetic and comparison MUST NOT operate on the raw string.
rule.rationale: Holding the value as a string past the validation boundary defeats the purpose — downstream code will eventually coerce to float and re-introduce drift.

rule.statement: Patterns MUST NOT be wider than necessary; reusing `^\\S+$` or `^.+$` for every string field adds zero decode-time constraint and earns false confidence.
rule.rationale: Wide patterns pass everything, so the schema looks "validated" but is not. Either pin the format precisely or omit the pattern keyword.

rule.statement: Every patterned field MUST have a `description` containing: (a) the format name, (b) a canonical example, (c) any edge case the model previously mis-handled.
rule.rationale: Patterns enforce; descriptions teach. Together they recover format compliance from ~92% to >99% on a 50-task eval.
